urn:contentItem:6JH6-TR23-RV7G-9382-00000-00Alabama solar developers object to municipal concerns (copy)2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisAGRICULTUREAlabama solar developers object to municipal concerns (copy)Sol Hauser

ALABAMA The developers of a proposed 550-acre, $117 million solar project that would span parts of Alabama, Pembroke, Oakfield and Batavia want to allay concerns about the project's impact on the land, as they seek state approval to override formal opposition from all four towns and from Genesee County.

In a sparsely attended informational session held last Thursday in South Alabama, representatives from Houston-based EDP Renewables North America a division of Portuguese utility Energias de Portugal detailed the planned layout and timeline of the proposed Alabama Solar Park.

The project, which the company expects to generate 130 megawatts of electricity annually, would occupy 550 acres of current farmland, bordered by the CSX railroad to the north, Maple Street Road to the west, Galloway Road to the south, and extending roughly 1,750 feet east of Hutton Road to the east.

The project would also include two temporary laydown yards used to store equipment during construction: one south of Hutton Road in Pembroke, near the corner of Maple Street Road, and one between Gorton and Wight roads in Alabama.

The project has drawn opposition from local governments, who have cited fears about the loss of usable farmland and potential irreversible damage once the solar panels are decommissioned, and staunchly opposed the state Office of Renewable Energy Siting's ability to override local laws restricting the construction of energy facilities.

Alabama's resolution which is identical in wording to those passed by several other Genesee County municipalities states that "these projects are consistently sited on highly productive farmland that has historically supported the local economy, food security, and the rural heritage of the region," and that "the conversion of such land to industrial energy production represents an irreversible loss of valuable agricultural resources."

The resolution continues, stating that "the use of state level siting mechanisms to override or circumvent local laws and planning documents, including comprehensive and agricultural protection plans, effectively strips municipalities and residents of their right to self-determination in land use matters," and that "such actions undermine the authority of local governments and erode the principle of 'home rule,' threatening democratic participation and the ability of communities to plan for their own futures."

Representatives for EDP say that the municipalities' concerns are unfounded.

The project's site was selected because of landowners' willingness to lease to EDP and the site's proximity to the National Grid-owned Lockport-Batavia power transmission line, said Project Manager Rachel Smith.

"You have to be able to put the electricity on the grid, so it has to be adjacent to a transmission line that is sufficient to take the energy," she said Thursday. "Secondly, you need to find willing landowners, and they have to connect."

Smith said EDP has performed extensive surveys of soils, wetlands, cultural resources and wildlife, and that the company expects state regulators to require more studies before approval.

Smith objected to the characterization of the solar infrastructure as permanent, pointing to EDP's commitment to funding a cleanup bond that would be held by the municipalities in case EDP became insolvent.

She also pointed to EDP's demolition and cleanup of the Madison Wind Farm in Central New York the first wind project built in New York, which the company determined had become obsolete and decommissioned last fall as evidence that the company stands by its commitments.

"We removed it in September of last year and started immediately with the restoration process, and we worked closely with the landowners to make sure that when we were taking them down, we were doing that in a way that would prevent or reduce compaction of soils," she said. "Then, we went and decompacted any soils that we needed to. Now, we're working closely hand in hand with the landowners to identify new fill material to kind of bring in, and then, as of this spring, we're doing final restoration. They'll be able to be farming that land this year."

EDP Community Resource Coordinator Katie Weaver, who is from Alabama, said that while the soils in the Alabama site may be classified as prime farmland by the U.S. Department of Agriculture, farmers she's talked to disagree with that assessment.

"We had a dinner last night, and one of the landowners' questions was, who gets to decide what is prime farmland?" Weaver said. "These landowners have struggled for years growing on this ground because there's so much clay and it's shallow and there are a lot of rocks in it, so it's been very difficult to farm. Even the landowners who aren't farmers and are renting it, they're not getting a lot of money for the ground because nothing grows on it very well. They're a little disgruntled that everyone's calling it prime farmland when it's so hard to grow things on it."

"The only thing that has really been grown on this ground is corn and soybeans, and some wheat," she continued. "There's no irrigation, and it's so dry that if you tried doing anything else, it would die. It's not really well suited for anything else and corn and soybeans are engineered to do better on dry soil, with heaps of fertilizer and herbicides and all this stuff. Coming from a farming family, I'm excited to see the ground rest and be able to build up organic matter, and to not have all the chemicals and give it time to break life cycles of pests and funguses. It'll be really good for the soil."

Currently, the Alabama Solar Park is in an early stage of development.

In the coming months, EDP is likely to file a notice of intent a letter providing 60 days' notice before the company files a formal application with the Office of Renewable Energy Siting. Once an application is submitted, ORES will respond within 60 days with a completeness determination indicating whether the project is ready to proceed, or whether more work needs to be done.

After ORES determines that the application is complete, the public will have at least 60 days to formally comment on the application, including at least 14 days to comment on ORES' planned decision. Within 30 days of the end of public comment and within a period no longer than 12 months from the completeness determination ORES must issue a final decision on whether to allow the project to proceed.

EDP anticipates that if Alabama Solar Park is approved as planned, construction will begin in 2030.

Maria Gallucci

May 12th, 2026 ( Canary Media - Delivered by Newstex )

Millions of inactive wells are littered across the United States, the relics of earlier eras of fossil fuel production. A large number of the sites have no official owner, and many are still polluting groundwater and leaking heat-trapping methane. The country has barely scratched the surface in dealing with this problem.

Policymakers in both Republican- and Democratic-led states are exploring whether these sites could instead be converted into new wells for producing geothermal energy. The holes are already drilled in the ground, after all. And regions with widespread oil and gas development have rich subsurface data that geothermal firms need in order to determine where and how to build their carbon-free systems.

The concept is relatively new and largely untested, though scientists and startups are working to change that. States are also laying the groundwork for action by lifting regulatory hurdles and launching in-depth studies.

In Oklahoma, the state Senate is considering a bill that would create a process for companies to buy abandoned oil and gas wells and repurpose them for geothermal energy or underground energy storage. Oklahoma has identified over 20,000 such wells, and state regulators estimate that it would take 235 years and hundreds of millions of dollars to plug all of them. Fixing a single old well can cost anywhere from $75,000 to $150,000 or more, by some calculations, depending on where it's located and how complicated it is to clean up.

The Well Repurposing Act, which passed Oklahoma's House in March, is modeled after a similar law that New Mexico adopted last year to address its 2,000-plus orphan wells.

The Oklahoma bill 'recognizes that these wells are a liability, and that there may be a way to turn them into some sort of revenue generation and give them value,' said Dave Tragethon, communications director for the nonprofit Well Done Foundation, which works to find and cap abandoned oil and gas wells nationwide. 'And if there's value, that means there's more of a willingness to address them and more of an opportunity to raise funding.'

In Alabama, legislators passed a law last month that allows the state to approve and regulate the conversion of oil and gas wells to tap alternative energy resources like geothermal. And in Colorado, state agencies just launched a technical study to evaluate the potential of repurposing old wells for geothermal development and carbon capture and sequestration.

These efforts reflect the growing bipartisan support for geothermal energy, which has largely remained unscathed by the Trump administration's efforts to block renewable energy projects. The energy resource has the potential to help meet the nation's soaring energy demand while also slashing planet-warming emissions from electricity and heating.

Converting wells is enticing but complicated

Geothermal systems work by circulating fluids underground to capture naturally occurring heat, which can then be used to drive turbines for generating electricity or to directly warm the air and water in buildings. The industry is gaining momentum thanks to recent advances in drilling methods and technologies that are making it technically possible or financially viable to access geothermal energy in more places.

Many of those breakthroughs have come from the oil and gas industry, whose skilled workforce of drilling engineers and geoscientists, and deep corporate pockets, have helped launch startups and deploy cutting-edge systems. However, most of that expertise and funding are being poured into building new projects - not figuring out how to retool leaky wells left behind by earlier generations.

'Oil and gas well conversion presents an enormous opportunity, but it's pretty far away technologically from being a reality,' said Emily Pope, a geologist and senior fellow at the Center for Climate and Energy Solutions who authored a recent study on next-generation geothermal power.

'There are some hurdles that are still pretty immense,' she said, adding that 'it is worth doing some R&D to try and grow.'

One of the biggest challenges is the fact that oil and gas wells tend to reach relatively low to medium underground temperatures. But high heat is key for geothermal projects, especially ones that generate electricity. The hotter the resource, the more energy a developer can wring out of the system.

Plus, fossil fuel wells generally produce smaller volumes of liquid and gas than geothermal wells need in order to spin power turbines or transfer heat to buildings. Geothermal operators might also have to take extra steps to keep nasty elements in the subsurface reservoirs from mixing with the working fluids used to extract heat underground, said Arash Dahi Taleghani, an engineering professor with the Repurposing Center for Energy Transition at Pennsylvania State University.

He added that the high cost of converting wells to geothermal has limited the number of real-world examples so far.

Early research efforts target direct-use heat and storage

At the University of Oklahoma, however, researchers have been evaluating how to turn four old oil and gas wells into sources of geothermal heat, which they hope to pipe into nearby public schools and homes in the city of Tuttle. The project was awarded a $1.7 million grant from the U.S. Department of Energy's Wells of Opportunity program in 2022, though it was paused last year during the Trump administration's sweeping freeze on federal funding and is still waiting to start its next phase, KGOU reported in March.

Saeed Salehi was the Oklahoma project's director before joining Southern Methodist University as an engineering professor in 2024. He said that repurposing wells for geothermal has several 'clear advantages.'

Geothermal firms can avoid significant upfront drilling costs if the wells are already sufficiently deep and hot enough. Oil and gas firms, which today pay millions of dollars to properly seal and shut down modern wells, can give their assets a second life instead. And communities near the aging fossil fuel infrastructure could benefit from having clean, affordable heat and lower winter utility bills.

'We need to collect enough data and have enough successful projects to take it to scale,' Salehi said, calling repurposed wells 'a custom solution for specific regions and areas.'

'Everything is going to take time, but I think we are moving in the right direction,' he added.

A smoother permitting process will be key to speeding things up, something Oklahoma, Alabama, and other states are aiming to address. States have traditionally lacked any regulatory framework for dealing with decades-old wells that no one is technically responsible for. Salehi said it took nearly nine months to get the Tuttle project's permits, though the process is growing faster now.

In Pennsylvania, Dahi Taleghani said his team is looking to secure funding to repurpose old wells to supply the Penn State campus with geothermal heating. They have also studied the potential for using some of the state's more than 200,000 abandoned wells to heat agricultural greenhouses, as well as to house energy-storage systems that compress air and stash it underground, acting as low-cost grid batteries.

'Decommissioning wells is expensive, costly, and it's not generating any revenue,' Dahi Taleghani said. 'So we're looking to [help] create businesses that can go after these leaky wells, fix them, and then use them for geothermal applications.'

value.Title: 20 Years After 'An Inconvenient Truth,' Al Gore Grapples With the (Big) Wrinkle of AI

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK1-KGG3-RRS4-X32T-00000-0020 Years After 'An Inconvenient Truth,' Al Gore Grapples With the (Big) Wrinkle of AI2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexis20 Years After 'An Inconvenient Truth,' Al Gore Grapples With the (Big) Wrinkle of AI

NASHVILLE, Tenn.-Former Vice President Al Gore is knocking on wood.

Seated in front of portraits of Johnny Cash and Prince inside The Pinnacle, a Nashville music venue, Gore told Inside Climate News that despite the current state of both national and global politics, he believes humanity can and will surmount the challenge of human-induced climate change. 

But that's not the whole story, he said. 

To meet the challenge presented by a rapidly warming world, Gore said it will take more than cheap talk. It will take action. 

Just before sitting down for a conversation at the beginning of May, Gore presented his climate change slideshow, its delivery perfected over two decades, to a room of hundreds of members of the Climate Reality Project, a Gore-led nonprofit that focuses on recruiting, training and mobilizing people to help speed the transition to a clean energy future. 

Now a network of over 4.5 million people globally, Gore said the nonprofit's trainees will be pivotal in moving forward the work of ending fossil fuel dependency and pushing toward a just transition to renewable energy.

Given the white-knuckled grasp of fossil fuel funders over public officials, he said, that transition won't happen without a struggle and won't happen overnight. Instead, it will take the commitment of Americans of all color, class and creed to secure the policy changes needed to ensure a stable future. 

Among the many challenges facing those concerned with curbing carbon emissions is the exponential explosion in planned energy generation and consumption associated with the AI boom. Across the country, communities have come together to oppose the power- and water-hungry data centers that power AI technology as the nation's tech behemoths dash to secure their place in the race for AI supremacy. 

In Alabama, for example, residents have almost universally opposed a data center development called Project Marvel in Bessemer, just outside Birmingham, that would lead to the clearcutting of hundreds of acres of agricultural land and, at full buildout, consume 1,200 megawatts of energy annually-enough electricity to power a million homes. That single development alone, just one of many data center proposals across Alabama, is slated to increase Alabama Power's demand for energy statewide by as much as 10 percent. 

Developments like Project Marvel are worrying, Gore acknowledged. 

"I read your reports," Gore said of Inside Climate News' reporting from Bessemer. "I think it is a real problem. I think that it's a problem on a national and global scale that is a cause for deep concern, but not panic."

Gore, now 78, said he isn't panicking for a few reasons. First, he said, while utilities in some regions of the U.S. may choose to fuel the data center boom with dirty energy, including Alabama Power, other utilities and some large tech companies themselves have committed to adopting renewables like wind and solar over fossil fuels. In 2025, giant tech companies like Google, Amazon, Microsoft and Meta accounted for about half of clean power purchase agreements globally, according to a report by BloombergNEF, though the report's authors also noted that corporate demand for clean energy had fallen 10 percent year over year, the first such decline in nearly a decade. 

While Gore said considering ways to reduce overall energy consumption is reasonable, it may be difficult to curb the surge in electricity demand fueled by AI data centers. 

"Yes, the underlying problem is in part that we are just continuing to use so much energy," Gore said. "But with this AI revolution, which is unlike any scientific revolution in prior history, it is going to be a real challenge to try and hold that back."

The construction of AI data centers across the country may be inevitable, Gore suggested, so the goal should be to encourage tech developers to co-locate renewables at data center sites. Gore said he prefers incentivizing the colocation of clean energy production to other strategies, including that of Maine's legislature, which recently passed a moratorium on new data centers in the state that was later vetoed by Gov. Janet Mills. 

And while the demand for energy spurred by AI has already led to significant fossil fuel investment, Gore said he's hopeful that AI tech can be deployed in specific ways to actually reduce carbon emissions.

Gore pointed to a recent study by researchers at the London School of Economics that concluded widespread adoption of AI tools as a means of reducing emissions could be transformative, potentially outweighing AI's energy costs. The study considered AI tools that could increase efficiencies in areas like grid management, food production and transportation and found that "the emissions reductions due to AI from just these three sectors alone would more than offset the estimated increase in emissions from all of AI's activities." 

Nicholas Stern, chair of the Grantham Research Institute and the paper's lead author, has warned, however, that the widespread adoption of AI as an emissions reduction tool is not inevitable. Without appropriate public policy, the energy costs of AI and resulting emissions are unlikely to be mitigated by the technology's emissions reductions potential. 

"Governments have a critical role in ensuring that AI is deployed effectively to accelerate the transition equitably and sustainably," Stern said. "Without active public policy, the commercial incentives to apply AI in the socially productive areas described may be weak."

A 2025 report by the International Energy Agency (IEA) was more explicitly skeptical: 

"It is vital to note that there is currently no momentum that could ensure the widespread adoption of these AI applications," the report concluded, referring to the use of renewable energy to power data centers. "Therefore, their aggregate impact, even in 2035, could be marginal if the necessary enabling conditions are not created."

Gore said he also believes that data center developers may find themselves without the public support necessary for such rapid growth. Recent polling suggests that significantly more Americans say they would oppose the construction of a data center in their neighborhood than say they would support it.

But in places like Alabama, near-universal opposition to data center developments has done little to slow the data center boom. Instead, public officials have glossed over residents' concerns, often pointing to the promise of tax revenue and job creation as paramount. 

Gore said he feels for residents in places like Bessemer who feel their concerns are being entirely dismissed. 

"I'm on their side," he said. "I understand their frustration and anger."

Ultimately, unresponsive elected officials are a result of an increasingly ineffective system, according to Gore-a system centered around pleasing donors, not representing the interests of the average American. 

"We have seen a capture of governmental processes in many areas with money playing way too big a role in the political process," he said. 

Still, Gore said he believes the writing is on the wall when it comes to public sentiment toward the way the Trump administration and many states are handling environmental issues. Since Trump's election, Gore said Americans have begun to better understand the consequences of the president's public policy preferences. 

"This president campaigned on 'No more wars in the Middle East. No more rising prices. We're going to bring inflation down,'" Gore said. "All the things he promised, he's doing the exact opposite."

Gore pointed to Trump's reported April 2024 meeting with oil executives where he asked for $1 billion in campaign funding. 

"He said 'Give me a billion dollars and I will do whatever you want-I'll get rid of regulations and laws that are not convenient for you,'" Gore said of Trump. "And they gave him the billion dollars, and we're seeing the transaction completed now."

Gore spoke faster and louder as he discussed the president's agenda. 

"I'm sorry," he said. "You triggered me there."

Americans aren't naive, Gore said. Come November, he believes Republicans may see the electoral chickens come home to roost. Gore rapped his knuckles on the table in front of him.

"Knock on wood," he said. 

Just two days before ICN's conversation with Gore, the U.S. Supreme Court's decision in Louisiana v. Callais had ignited a rush to eliminate majority-minority districts across the South. The day of the interview, state legislatures in Alabama and Tennessee were called into special sessions to solidify any possible advantage for the GOP in November's midterm elections. 

The last-minute election map changes across the country spurred by the high court's intervention may become an obstacle to adopting sound climate policy. 

"I think it's a problem for democracy," Gore said. "And in order to solve the climate crisis, we're going to have to address the democracy crisis. This is a move very much in the wrong direction where democracy is concerned."

Before the end of the conversation, Gore touched on America's lurching role in solving the climate crisis. Unfortunately, he said, the Trump administration has eroded our ability to lead on issues of climate. In some ways, the dynamic has left China with more international credibility on the issue than the United States, according to Gore. 

"That's because the United States is led by someone who is profoundly dishonest and is the most corrupt president in all of American history," Gore said. "Case closed."

While China's continued investment in coal is a "demerit" for the country, Gore explained, analysts expect that the Chinese government will continue to lead the world in the buildout of solar and wind capacity. 

"Once they [shift away from coal], most expectations are that China's emissions are going to decline quite dramatically for a long period of time," he said. 

Whether in China or the United States, Gore said that solving the climate crisis remains a challenge but that we have the available technology to get the job done. The barrier remains in the political sphere, where the political influence of the fossil fuel industry continues to put a stranglehold on efforts to quicken a just energy transition. 

That barrier is evident now more than ever in the South, Gore said, where legislative majorities are sometimes effectively extensions of the fossil fuel lobby itself. 

"The fossil fuel industry is in league with the Trump administration and the governments of a number of southern states in particular," Gore said. "And they have so much power that they just keep politicians under their thumb and tell them what to do, and they say, 'Yes, sir,' and do it."

But those interested in the future of American democracy-and, for that matter, life on Earth-failure is not an option, Gore said. 

"I have 10 grandchildren, but no greats yet," Gore said, a smile curling on his face. "We have the opportunity to shape the future. I would prefer to shape it into a clean, egalitarian future for everyone."

Damage-some irreversible-has already been done to the Earth's climate, Gore said, and worse is yet to come. But the most dire changes aren't inevitable, either. 

"We can still prevent the most catastrophic changes that are feared," Gore said. "But right now we're running a reckless risk of crossing the tipping point that would make the future a worse time for our grandchildren. Let's change that."

value.Title: Fayette Twp. commissioners again delay decision on Heartwood Solar II

urn:contentItem:6JJG-GDB3-S02K-S0T3-00000-00Fayette Twp. commissioners again delay decision on Heartwood Solar II2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisFayette Twp. commissioners again delay decision on Heartwood Solar IICorey J. Murray, Hillsdale Daily News

FAYETTE TWP. — As officials reopened a public hearing on Heartwood Solar II from Ranger Power on May 7, Fayette Township Planning Commission Chairperson Jane Munson reminded residents to behave with decorum and respect.

Her words stemmed from a written complaint from an attendee who felt physically threatened during the ongoing public debate.

“We’ve had a lot of verbal abuse,” Munson said. “Everybody has a right to their opinion, but that opinion needs to be stated in a non-vicious way. We ask that you treat everyone with respect.”

Munson said anyone unable to control their behavior could be removed.

Link to Image

The public hearing continuation lasted just over two hours May 7, with officials noting increased tension surrounding the large-scale solar proposal. Heartwood II has faced sustained opposition since being introduced by Ranger Power — a national renewable energy developer — as an expansion of an existing utility-scale solar development on the other side of the municipality.

The project would cover more than 1,000 additional acres of agricultural land with solar panels, leading residents to raise concerns about farmland preservation, property values, drainage and long-term impacts on the rural community.

During the most recent meeting, public comment again focused overwhelmingly on opposition, with residents urging commissioners to reject the proposal outright and challenge PA 233, which transfers decision power over certain solar, wind and battery energy storage proposals to the Michigan Public Service Commission.

In previous presentations, Ranger Power has said the project would generate clean energy capable of powering thousands of homes, as well as tax revenue for local governments and schools.

When comment concluded, commissioners against voted to postpone a decision. It wasn't immediately clear when discussions will resume.

The Fayette Township Board is scheduled to meet at 7 p.m. May 13 at Jonesville Middle School, though a recommendation from the commission must come before a final decision from trustees.

— Contact reporter Corey Murray at cmurray@hillsdale.ne t or follow him on X, formerly Twitter, at @cmurrayhdn.

urn:contentItem:6JHK-FMF3-SB7X-62KP-00000-00Idaho Power wants to build two natural gas power plants. Here's where and why2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisCONSTRUCTION NEWS, Oil and gas news & UTILITIES NEWSIdaho Power wants to build two natural gas power plants. Here's where and whyAngela Palermo

Idaho Power is asking state regulators for permission to build two natural gas power plants in SouthernIdaho to meet fast-growing demand for electricity in its service area.

The company says it needs the plants to avoid capacity shortfalls expected to begin in 2029.

It plans to build the South Hills Power Plant in Twin Falls County and the Peregrine Power Plant in Elmore County. Idaho Power says the natural gas-fueled plants are the cheapest and least-risky option to address the anticipated capacity deficits.

The South Hills plant, estimated to become operational in mid-2029, is designed to provide 222 megawatts of electricity. The Peregrine plant, to become operational in mid-2030, would provide 430 megawatts of electricity. Idaho Power submitted an application seeking certificates of public necessity to build the plants, according to a news release from the Idaho Public Utilities Commission.

"We're responding to continued rapid growth across our service area and leaning into a diverse resource mix that will enable us to continue providing reliable, affordable service," Brad Bowlin, a spokesperson for Idaho Power, told the Idaho Statesman by email.

Hydropower, a renewable energy source that generates electricity via flowing water, remains the company's largest single energy source, accounting for about 37% of the total energy delivered to Idaho Power customers in 2025, though that amount can vary depending on water conditions and other factors, Bowlin said.

Natural gas made up nearly 23% of the company's energy mix, and wind and solar were about 18% combined.

Idaho Power is phasing out of coal altogether, planning to finish transitioning its Jim Bridger Power Plant in Wyoming to natural gas by the end of 2030. Two of the four generating units at the plant have already been converted, Bowlin said.

"Gas-fired power generates significantly less CO2 and nearly eliminates other emissions relative to coal-fired plants," he said.

The natural gas power plants it hopes to build in SouthernIdaho are subject to approval by the commission. Idaho Power didn't propose any rate increases in its application.

If the projects are approved, customer rates wouldn't be affected until the plants are in service. But several factors could influence the timing and magnitude of future rate changes. Bowlin said the company isn't able to provide an estimate of how customer rates might eventually be impacted.

Once the plants come online, they would be the most efficient natural gas-powered units on Idaho Power's system.

"We expect to use them on a regular basis," Bowlin said. "Natural gas is highly flexible with its ability to come online quickly and ramp up or down as needed, so it will complement the existing system very well. Idaho Power also has significant hydro, solar, wind, and storage resources, which will be maximized first."

The plants would be particularly useful for meeting peak demand in the winter months, when solar and batteries are not as effective.

Idaho Power has added 460 megawatts of solar capacity to its system since 2023, with another 225 megawatts scheduled to come online this year and next. The company has also added more than 450 megawatts of battery storage, and plans for about 350 megawatts of additional capacity as customer demand continues going up.

The company operates 17 hydropower facilities on the Snake River and its tributaries, and also buys hydropower seasonally from 68 small-scale projects across its service area, according to Bowlin.

"While additional hydro power would be great, Idaho Power does not see any feasible additional hydro opportunities in the near future," he said. "Natural gas resources are ideal for adjusting to the variability of those renewable sources to ensure we continue to meet our customers' need for safe, reliable, affordable energy."

value.Title: Southern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-KSM3-S0XP-40MH-00000-00Southern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisSouthern Company Q1 2026 Operational Highlights and Key Risks *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

This article includes relevant portions of a research report that we wrote and published analyzing Southern Company's Q1 2026 10-Q filing, which was made available following the company's earnings release on April 30, 2026.

INDEX

SECTION 1 SECTION 1 Primary Risk Factors Analysis

SECTION 2 Financials as reported (Quarter ending 31 March 2026)

SECTION 1 SECTION 1 Primary Risk Factors Analysis

Regulatory and Legislative Changes

- Potential Impact: Evolving federal and state legislative frameworks, particularly stringent environmental regulations governing coal combustion residuals (CCR), shifts in corporate tax policy, and modifications to established rate recovery mechanisms, pose a foundational structural risk to the fundamental cost frameworks and forward-looking capital deployment strategies of the enterprise. The operational entities face materially increased compliance expenditures directly associated with ongoing and future CCR management and asset retirement obligations. Furthermore, potential legislative alterations or restrictive interpretations concerning production tax credits (PTCs) and investment tax credits (ITCs) under the statutory frameworks of the OBBB could severely limit the eligibility of the enterprise for tax-advantaged financing on renewable energy projects, particularly those projects whose supply chains or capital structures are influenced by foreign entities of concern. On the state level, ongoing and upcoming rate cases alongside regulatory proceedings adjudicated by Public Service Commissions (PSCs) in Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi possess a direct, material influence over the organization's overarching ability to successfully and timely recover substantial capital expenditures. These capital outflows are primarily allocated toward new generation capacity, comprehensive transmission grid modernization, and essential storm damage restoration efforts. Any administrative delay or regulatory denial in the subsequent rate recovery for these large-scale capital investments-particularly those necessitated by the rapid load growth driven by the expansion of hyperscale data centers-possesses the potential to severely compress authorized returns on equity (ROE) and strain consolidated operating cash flows. Additionally, statutory or regulatory restrictions imposed on base rate increases, such as the mandated restrictions specifically governing operations within the state of Alabama through the year 2028, mechanically limit the enterprise's pricing flexibility and revenue realization amidst an environment characterized by broadly rising macroeconomic and operational costs.

- Investor Assessment: Institutional investors and fixed-income allocators must rigorously monitor ongoing state PSC dockets alongside evolving federal guidance from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the Department of the Treasury. The pervasive regulatory lag associated with cost recovery mechanisms represents a core earnings and cash flow risk, particularly given the heavy construction and capital expenditure cycle currently underway across the operating footprint. This specific risk vector is distinctly heightened by the capital-intensive nature inherently required to meet projected geometric demand growth while simultaneously attempting to maintain investment-grade credit metrics and balance sheet health.

Construction and Project Execution Risks

- Potential Impact: The Southern Company system is presently navigating a period of significantly elevated capital spending concentrated on expanding generation capacity-including natural gas turbines, utility-scale battery storage installations, and nuclear facility lifecycle extensions-as well as comprehensive transmission and distribution network upgrades. Unforeseen schedule delays, systemic cost overruns, or a failure to successfully obtain necessary environmental permits and regulatory certifications in a timely manner could materially inflate Construction Work in Progress (CWIP) asset balances and correspondingly elevate carrying and financing costs. Specific, identifiable execution risks natively include ongoing macroeconomic supply chain disruptions, skilled labor shortages within specialized construction trades, adverse severe weather events impacting project sites, judicial or regulatory challenges levied by adversarial parties against approved projects, and complex systemic integration issues upon the commissioning of new generation units. Furthermore, Southern Power's active portfolio of wind repowering initiatives and greenfield solar projects inherently carries substantial accelerated depreciation exposure until the respective assets reach their formal Commercial Operation Date (COD). The integration of large load contracts, primarily driven by the proliferation of regional data centers, additionally introduces acute revenue recovery uncertainty in scenarios where the actual megawatt load ramps at a fundamentally slower velocity than initially projected in the underlying offtake models.

- Investor Assessment: The current operational profile dictates that this is a highly execution-heavy risk period for the enterprise. Investors are advised to actively track the accumulation of Allowance for Funds Used During Construction (AFUDC) trends, the velocity of CWIP balance conversions to in-service utility plant, and the cadence of administrative regulatory approvals corresponding to major capital projects, such as Georgia Power's Integrated Resource Plan (IRP) resource allocations. Persistent, unmitigated cost overruns across the construction portfolio possess the capacity to exert downward pressure on consolidated credit ratings and challenge long-term dividend payout sustainability.

Fuel Price and Supply Volatility

- Potential Impact: Material fluctuations in the prevailing market prices for natural gas, thermal coal, and wholesale purchased power directly and immediately affect aggregate operating expenses across the portfolio of traditional regulated utilities. While established statutory fuel recovery clauses and pass-through mechanisms generally provide for the ultimate recovery of these commodity costs, the structural timing mismatches between expense realization and rate recovery, coupled with mandatory regulatory prudence reviews, systematically create episodic cash flow friction and observable earnings variability. Southern Company Gas explicitly faces direct commodity price exposure and basis risk across its gas marketing services and commercial storage operations. Furthermore, structurally higher underlying commodity prices can adversely impact aggregate customer affordability metrics, thereby introducing demand elasticity risks and potentially elevating bad debt expense allocations.

- Mitigation Strategies: Fuel recovery mechanisms and contractual pass-through frameworks are actively utilized to mitigate direct exposure to underlying commodity price volatility.

- Investor Assessment: While established fuel recovery mechanisms insulate the core earnings profile, they do not entirely eliminate working capital friction or overarching regulatory risk. Investors must diligently monitor wholesale natural gas price volatility and its downstream translation into aggregate customer retail bills, as a prolonged period of elevated energy prices possesses the potential to either accelerate structural consumer electrification trends or catalyze adverse regulatory and political pushback regarding affordability.

Demand Growth and Large Load Uncertainty

- Potential Impact: The enterprise is projecting significant forward electricity demand growth, catalyzed primarily by the rapid regional deployment of hyperscale data centers and broader industrial electrification initiatives. This demand trajectory necessitates the immediate, substantial deployment of new capital toward both generation capacity and transmission infrastructure. Associated risks primarily manifest as a slower-than-expected customer facility ramp-up, premature offtake contract terminations, or a structural failure to adequately recover the incremental infrastructural costs through approved rate designs. This dynamic effectively creates acute capital access constraints and Return on Equity (ROE) pressure in the event that the projected load fails to materialize exactly as contractually forecasted.

- Mitigation Strategies: The executed large load contracts are structured to include various downside risk protections, including mandatory minimum billing thresholds and upfront capital contributions from the counterparties.

- Investor Assessment: This dynamic represents a bifurcated vector of both immense growth opportunity and substantial operational risk. While downside protections are structurally embedded within the counterparties' agreements, the tangible execution risk remains elevated. Financial modeling should continuously monitor the variance between actual megawatt load additions and the originally contracted volume estimates, directly cross-referencing these figures against approved rate base growth trajectories.

Cybersecurity and Physical Security Threats

- Potential Impact: The threat landscape encompasses potential sophisticated cyber intrusions or coordinated physical attacks targeting the regional electric grid, baseload and peaking generation facilities, or critical natural gas transmission infrastructure. A successful breach or physical disruption could directly precipitate widespread operational disruptions, critical safety incidents, sensitive internal or customer data breaches, and the subsequent imposition of severe regulatory penalties. The compounding digitization of core utility operations, alongside an increasing structural reliance on decentralized third-party software and service vendors, materially heightens the aggregate attack surface and operational exposure.

- Investor Assessment: This exposure categorizes as an enterprise-wide systemic risk possessing the distinct potential to cause both significant, prolonged service outages and permanent reputational degradation. The investor base should analytically evaluate the enterprise's ongoing capital allocation toward grid modernization, digital hardening, and cybersecurity infrastructure as a foundational, core resiliency metric governing long-term asset security.

Interest Rate and Financing Risks

- Potential Impact: An environment characterized by rising nominal interest rates inherently increases the prevailing cost of new debt issuance and directly impacts the calculation of AFUDC credits across the construction portfolio. Structurally higher financing costs required to fund the expansive, multi-year large capital deployment program possess the capacity to exert mechanical pressure on target credit metrics and ultimately elevate customer retail rates. Additionally, the mandatory refinancing of existing maturing debt tranches at higher prevailing market yields mathematically adds to the ongoing interest expense burden, directly impacting the bottom line.

- Investor Assessment: Given the presence of significant outstanding long-term debt liabilities combined with substantial ongoing external borrowing requirements, the enterprise's exposure to interest rate volatility is considered highly material. Analysts must continuously track Funds from Operations to Total Debt (FFO/Debt) ratios alongside the spread between the regulatory allowed ROEs and the enterprise's incrementally rising weighted average cost of capital (WACC).

Environmental and Climate-Related Risks

- Potential Impact: The implementation of increasingly strict CCR management rules, the potential introduction of punitive carbon pricing or regulatory emissions caps, and the direct physical risks stemming from extreme weather events-specifically coastal hurricanes and severe inland storms-possess the capability to drive materially higher ongoing compliance expenditures and acute post-storm infrastructure restoration costs. Transition risks encompass the financial and operational friction associated with mandated systemic shifts away from traditional natural gas and coal-fired generation profiles, particularly if legislative policy artificially accelerates broad electrification goals or enforces stringent renewable portfolio mandates prior to economic parity.

- Investor Assessment: While the enterprise's systematically balanced generation mix and the presence of established regulatory recovery mechanisms provide a measurable degree of financial buffering, unmitigated physical climate risks remain a recurring and highly unpredictable driver of forward earnings and operating cash flow volatility.

Litigation and Legal Proceedings

- Potential Impact: The enterprise remains subject to ongoing judicial matters, which prominently include complex antitrust litigation specifically related to historical nuclear workforce compensation practices, as well as aggressive environmental citizen suits targeting operational CCR facilities. Adverse judgments in these arenas could ultimately result in the imposition of substantial monetary damages, restrictive injunctive relief, or legally mandated, costly alterations to established daily operating practices.

- Investor Assessment: While standard accounting provisions and balance sheet reserves exist to address probable losses, severely adverse outcomes in these high-profile key cases could precipitate direct financial and operational impacts that significantly exceed current baseline reserve allocations.

urn:contentItem:6JHD-M503-S6WK-12HV-00000-00How Shake Shack Ranks Among Companies with 2025 Investments Exceeding $1 Billion *Updated*2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:36:31ZLexisNexisHow Shake Shack Ranks Among Companies with 2025 Investments Exceeding $1 Billion *Updated*

NYSE STOCK REPORT

During President Trump's second term, corporate investment in the United States has increased, with major companies in technology, manufacturing, and healthcare allocating billions to domestic expansion. The semiconductor industry has committed over $500 billion in private-sector funding, indicating a notable reshoring trend. This investment activity is backed by enhanced tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, including 100% bonus depreciation and 35% manufacturing credits, and tariff policies aimed at supporting domestic production.

The reshoring trend affects multiple high-value sectors. Semiconductor manufacturing investments of over $500 billion across U.S. facilities are expected to create more than 150,000 new jobs, while pharmaceutical companies have allocated over $150 billion for U.S. manufacturing investments. Foreign direct investment was recorded at $102 billion in Q2 2025, representing a 137% increase from Q1. This comprehensive restructuring is influenced by both policy incentives and supply chain resilience priorities.

This report examines investment commitments from companies across the technology, manufacturing, healthcare, and energy sectors.

Activities

Shake Shack Inc., a New York Stock Exchange-listed company (NYSE: SHAK), is a fast-casual restaurant chain renowned for its high-quality burgers, fries, shakes, and other American classics, emphasizing fresh ingredients and a welcoming community vibe. Founded in 2004 as a hot dog cart in Manhattan, the company has evolved into a global brand operating and franchising over 400 locations across the U.S. and international markets. Its core business activities center on delivering premium fast-casual dining experiences, with a focus on sustainable sourcing, innovative menu development, and efficient operations. The fastest-growing segments include international expansion, particularly in Europe and Asia, where new outlets have driven revenue gains, as well as digital channels like mobile ordering and delivery partnerships, which have surged amid rising consumer demand for convenience. This strategic growth underscores Shake Shack's adaptability in a competitive food service landscape, solidifying its position as a leader in elevated fast-casual dining. It is the NYSE's 7th largest Restaurant & bar company by market capitalisation.

Ascend Collection 500-property milestone marks a significant achievement for Choice Hotels International, Inc., NYSE:CHH. Celebrating the Ascend Collection milestone, this boutique soft brand strengthens its position in the realm of Ascend Collection independent hotels. Concurrently, Choice Hotels has revolutionized its Sleep Inn brand with the Sleep Inn Scenic Dreams design and the Sleep Inn new Scenic Dreams design. Both Sleep Inn prototype and the Sleep Inn new prototype design underscore the brand's commitment to evolving guest experiences. This initiative aligns with the Sleep Inn prototype rollout and Sleep Inn midscale redesign, enhancing the Sleep Inn guest experience.

Parallelly, Choice Hotels AWS partnership has catalyzed Choice Hotels AWS AI integration and Choice Hotels AI integration across operations. Choice Hotels cloud infrastructure AWS and Choice Hotels cloud migration AWS exemplify their focus on intelligent responsive hotel operations, emphasizing cloud-based hospitality operations and hotel operations automation Choice Hotels. The Choice Hotels technology partnership promotes comprehensive Choice Hotels enterprise AI adoption. This, coupled with the Choice Hotels portfolio expansion and franchising opportunities, enables the Ascend Collection global expansion and hotel brand expansion Ascend Collection, reinforcing the Choice Hotels property milestone 500 in hospitality prototype design and hotel design innovation Choice Hotels, particularly in franchised hotel technology integration.
